title: The Best New Bars in Miami
summary: As a gateway between the U.S., the Caribbean, and Latin America, Miami has long been a champion of diverse flavors — rum and agave spirits mingling with notes of citrus and exotic fruits. Recently, vermouth and sherry-based drinks, along with classic Spanish cocktails and…
whatHappening: Miami's bar scene is experiencing a creative renaissance, with new establishments showcasing vermouth and sherry-based cocktails alongside classic Spanish drinks. The city's mixology landscape is evolving beyond its traditional rum and agave spirit foundations.
whoInvolved: Eater Miami is covering the trend, spotlighting local bartenders and bar owners who are curating these new venues. These establishments represent a mix of independent operators and emerging hospitality concepts in the Miami market.
whyMatters: The shift reflects broader consumer interest in sophisticated, heritage-driven cocktail culture and opens opportunities for bars to differentiate themselves in a competitive market. This trend positions Miami as a destination for diverse spirits tourism and elevated drinking experiences beyond typical vacation cocktails.
moreContext: Miami's geographic position as a cultural crossroads between the U.S., Caribbean, and Latin America has historically shaped its beverage culture around rum and agave spirits infused with tropical citrus and exotic fruits. The current emphasis on European spirits like vermouth and sherry signals bartenders are drawing inspiration from Spanish and Mediterranean cocktail traditions. This represents a maturation of Miami's cocktail scene, moving from spirit-forward tropical drinks to more balanced, nuanced libations. Industry-wide, vermouth has experienced a global renaissance among craft cocktail bars over the past five years, and Miami venues are now embracing this trend locally.

title: Where to Drink the Best Coffee in Miami
summary: Coffee and pastries from Panther Coffee. | Panther Coffee Miami has long been known for excellent Cuban coffee, but the city’s specialty coffee scene took a little longer to find its footing. These days, the Magic City boasts an ever-growing collection of standout coffeehouses…
whatHappening: Miami's coffee scene is experiencing a renaissance beyond its traditional Cuban coffee roots. The city now hosts a thriving collection of specialty coffeehouses offering high-quality craft beverages and pastries alongside classic Cuban offerings.
whoInvolved: Panther Coffee, a Miami-based coffeehouse, has been instrumental in establishing the city's specialty coffee reputation. The establishment has built its brand on excellence in Cuban coffee while helping to pioneer the broader specialty coffee movement in the region.
whyMatters: Miami's evolving coffee culture signals the city's growing sophistication as a culinary destination and its ability to blend traditional flavors with modern cafe trends. This diversification attracts both locals and tourists seeking quality beverages, strengthening the hospitality economy.
moreContext: Miami has historically been defined by its Cuban coffee traditions, a cultural cornerstone for decades. The specialty coffee movement—characterized by single-origin beans, precise brewing methods, and artisanal preparation—arrived later to Miami than other major U.S. cities but has now taken hold. Panther Coffee's success in maintaining Cuban coffee excellence while embracing specialty techniques demonstrates how the city is honoring its heritage while modernizing. This dual approach has created a unique Miami coffee identity that distinguishes it from other American coffee hubs.

title: Eating News in Miami, July 2026
summary: A different kind of tea time -- tipsy tea, an unbeatable offer for mango lovers, new high profile restaurant in Coral Gables, ladies night with all the perks, a new waterfront option for cocktails and delicious comfort food from leading chefs, decadent ice cream and churros,…
whatHappening: Miami's dining and beverage scene is experiencing a summer surge in July 2026 with multiple new food and drink concepts launching simultaneously. From tipsy tea experiences to waterfront cocktail bars and collaborations with renowned chefs, the city is rolling out diverse culinary offerings targeting different demographics and occasions.
whoInvolved: Miami Curated is highlighting multiple hospitality venues and chefs operating across Miami neighborhoods, particularly in Coral Gables and waterfront areas. The scene includes both established culinary names expanding into new concepts and emerging venues capitalizing on Miami's status as a major food and beverage destination.
whyMatters: This cluster of new openings signals strong post-summer recovery and confidence in Miami's hospitality sector, which typically experiences seasonal fluctuations. The diversity of concepts—from ladies' nights to chef-driven comfort food—indicates operators are targeting multiple consumer segments to maximize year-round traffic during traditionally slower summer months.
moreContext: Miami has positioned itself as a premier dining destination over the past decade, with Coral Gables serving as a hub for upscale restaurants and hospitality venues. The summer season typically sees reduced tourism, but local promotions like ladies' nights and themed experiences (tipsy tea, waterfront lounging) are designed to maintain customer engagement. The emergence of multiple openings suggests competitive saturation in Miami's dining market, with venues differentiating through specialized experiences rather than traditional fine dining alone.

title: From Casual Lunch to Serious Dinner: Paperfish Sushi Brickell Stepped Up Its Game
summary: Paperfish Sushi Brickell has always been one of my “go to” places to have lunch or happy hour with a friend and they always like it. With all of the sushi restaurants in town you’d ask why Paperfish Sushi? The lovely décor that brightens up your day the moment you walk in,…
whatHappening: Paperfish Sushi Brickell has elevated its restaurant concept beyond casual lunch and happy hour destination to offer serious dinner experiences. The establishment is leveraging its existing strengths—including attractive décor and customer loyalty—to compete in Miami's crowded sushi market.
whoInvolved: Paperfish Sushi Brickell is a sushi restaurant operating in Miami's Brickell neighborhood. The venue has built a reputation as a reliable spot for casual daytime dining and after-work drinks.
whyMatters: This expansion signals a strategic move to capture higher-margin dinner service and compete with upscale dining options in the competitive Miami sushi scene. Successfully positioning itself as a serious dinner destination could drive increased revenue and customer retention beyond its existing lunch and happy hour base.
moreContext: Miami's dining landscape includes numerous sushi restaurants, making differentiation challenging for individual establishments. Paperfish has historically succeeded by offering appealing ambiance and consistent quality at the casual dining level. The shift toward dinner service suggests the restaurant is responding to market demand and seeking to maximize its venue's potential across different dayparts and customer segments. This reflects a broader trend among casual concept restaurants in major metros attempting to capture both quick-service and fine-dining occasions.

title: New Places to Shop, Eat in the Miami Design District
summary: With the crowds having gone from the Dolce & Gabbana show and many from FIFA, now is a good time to make a visit to the Miami Design District, especially since new shops have opened that are brands that are probably new to you as well as restaurants. Plus, you might come across…
whatHappening: The Miami Design District is experiencing a surge of new retail and dining openings, making it an ideal time to visit now that major events like the Dolce & Gabbana show and FIFA have concluded. Fresh shops featuring emerging and established brands are opening alongside new restaurants, revitalizing the neighborhood's commercial landscape.
whoInvolved: The Miami Design District, a premier shopping and dining destination in Miami, is hosting new retailers and restaurants. Luxury brand Dolce & Gabbana and FIFA events recently drew crowds to the area, establishing it as a major venue for fashion and international events.
whyMatters: The influx of new businesses strengthens the Miami Design District's position as a must-visit destination for shoppers and diners seeking unique, curated experiences. For the local economy, these openings drive foot traffic, create jobs, and attract tourism during a period following major cultural events that boost the area's profile.
moreContext: The Miami Design District has established itself as a hub for luxury shopping, art, and dining in recent years. The area attracts international attention through fashion events like designer shows and major sporting events such as FIFA. The timing of these new openings capitalizes on heightened visitor interest and foot traffic generated by recent high-profile events. Smart retailers are leveraging this momentum to debut new concepts when consumer attention is already focused on the neighborhood.

title: PASTA: The Model Miami Restaurant
summary: Very reasonable prices, excellent food across the board, very good service, low noise decibel so you can easily talk, PASTA is a dream restaurant which should serve as a model to others in Miami. I'll even go so far as to say it has the best pasta in Miami. And on top of all…
whatHappening: PASTA restaurant in Miami is being recognized as an exemplary dining establishment that combines excellent food quality with reasonable pricing and exceptional service. The restaurant has earned praise for offering what critics describe as the best pasta in Miami while maintaining a refined, low-noise atmosphere conducive to conversation.
whoInvolved: PASTA is a Miami-based restaurant reviewed by Miami Curated, a local food and lifestyle publication. The establishment operates in Miami's competitive restaurant scene where fine dining typically commands premium prices.
whyMatters: PASTA demonstrates that high-quality dining doesn't require excessive prices or pretension, setting a standard for how other Miami restaurants should operate. This model challenges the perception that premium food experiences must come with inflated costs and loud, chaotic atmospheres, potentially influencing dining culture in the city.
moreContext: Miami's restaurant landscape has traditionally featured high-end establishments with premium pricing and trendy, often loud environments. PASTA's success with reasonable prices, quality ingredients, and attentive service suggests a growing demand for more accessible fine dining. The emphasis on low noise levels and conversational comfort reflects broader dining trends prioritizing experience quality over exclusivity. Local food critics increasingly evaluate restaurants on value proposition alongside culinary execution, making PASTA's balanced approach particularly noteworthy.

title: A Day in Sunset Harbour Miami
summary: Sunset Harbour Miami is unique. It is one of Miami Beach’s most popular, vibrant, and walkable neighborhoods. Located on the quieter bay side of South Beach, it trades the chaotic tourist energy of Ocean Drive for a chic, wellness-focused, local lifestyle. It packs a big…
whatHappening: Sunset Harbour is establishing itself as Miami Beach's premier walkable neighborhood, offering an alternative to the tourist-heavy Ocean Drive scene. The bay-side community is cultivating a lifestyle centered on wellness and local culture rather than chaotic tourism.
whoInvolved: Miami Curated is highlighting Sunset Harbour, a neighborhood within Miami Beach that caters to residents and visitors seeking a more refined, community-oriented experience. The area comprises local businesses, wellness establishments, and hospitality venues that prioritize quality over volume.
whyMatters: This shift reflects changing preferences among Miami Beach visitors and residents who value authentic local experiences over mass tourism. The emergence of wellness-focused neighborhoods could reshape how Miami Beach positions itself as a destination and influence real estate and business development patterns across the region.
moreContext: Sunset Harbour benefits from its location on the bay side of South Beach, which naturally separates it from the high-energy Ocean Drive corridor known for nightlife and large crowds. The neighborhood represents a growing trend in major tourist destinations where quieter, lifestyle-oriented districts attract affluent visitors and long-term residents. This positioning allows Sunset Harbour to command premium pricing while building a distinct brand identity. The shift also reflects broader post-pandemic preferences for outdoor, wellness-focused activities and neighborhood walkability over traditional nightlife-centric tourism.

title: Taste the Gables is Back with 75 Restaurants
summary: The City of Coral Gables’ popular restaurant month, Taste the Gables, returns this July with its most expansive lineup to date. More than 75 restaurants, ranging from Michelin-recognized destinations to neighborhood favorites and varied cuisines, are participating, showcasing…
whatHappening: Coral Gables' popular restaurant promotion Taste the Gables is returning this July with its largest roster yet. Over 75 restaurants are participating, spanning Michelin-recognized fine dining establishments to casual neighborhood spots across diverse cuisines.
whoInvolved: The City of Coral Gables is organizing the event, partnering with restaurants ranging from upscale destinations to local favorites throughout the city. These establishments represent Miami's dining diversity and culinary prestige.
whyMatters: The expanded event drives foot traffic and revenue to local restaurants during summer, traditionally a slower dining season. For diners, it offers an accessible way to experience high-end and new restaurants at promotional prices, while boosting Coral Gables' reputation as a culinary destination.
moreContext: Taste the Gables has become an annual fixture in Coral Gables' cultural calendar, similar to restaurant weeks in other major cities. The event capitalizes on the dining industry's post-pandemic recovery momentum and increasing consumer interest in experiential dining events. The inclusion of Michelin-recognized venues alongside neighborhood restaurants signals the city's efforts to celebrate both culinary excellence and community dining culture. July timing targets the summer season when special promotions help maintain restaurant traffic during typically slower months.
